coredata.dc:title: Small nitrogen-doped carbon dots as efficient nanoenhancer for boosting the electrochemical performance of three-dimensional graphene
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Gang
Zhao, Xiao
Liang, Yeru
Peng, Lin
Dong, Hanwu
Xiao, Yong
Hu, Chaofan
Hu, Hang
Liu, Yingliang
Zheng, Mingtao
coredata.dc:description: As a new class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots have triggered intensive research interest in various fields. However, the low surface area, hydrophilicity, and agglomeration characteristics limit their applications in energy storage fields. Herein, we demonstrate that nitrogen-doped carbon dots can be employed as efficient nanoenhancer to boost the electrochemical performance of three-dimensional graphene. The as-prepared materials exhibit an interconnected framework with abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups, which enable fast penetration and transport of electrolyte ions and provide more active sites and electric conductivity. Employed as binder-free electrode for supercapacitors, the resultant materials present high specific capacitance (338 F g−1) and areal capacitance (604 μF cm−2) at a current density of 0.5 A g−1, which is much higher than that of pristine three-dimensional graphene (190 F g−1, and 114 μF cm−2), with an enhancement of 78% and 430%, respectively. Moreover, superior long-term cycling stability (94% of capacitance retention after 20 000 charging/discharging cycles at 10 A g−1) as well as improved electric conductivity can also be achieved. These results certify that nitrogen-doped carbon dots can be applied as nanobooster to comprehensively improve the performance of graphene for high-performance electrochemical energy storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Graphene
Nanoenhancer
Supercapacitors

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21571066 , U1501242 , 51602107 , and 21671069 ), the Key Program of Science Technology Innovation Foundation of Universities ( cxzd1113 ), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province ( S2013030012842 ), and Guangdong Science and Technology Planning Project ( 2015A020209147 , and 201704030022 ) in China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescence property and morphology of the as-prepared N-CDs. (a) Emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths and (b) The excitation spectrum (monitored at 430 nm) and emission spectrum (excited at 350 nm) of N-CDs. Inset in b shows the photographs of N-CDs in daylight (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right). (c) TEM and (d) AFM images of N-CDs. Inset in c and d displays the HRTEM image and the size distribution curve, respectively.

SEM images of 3DG (a), N-CDs/3DG-1 (b), N-CDs/3DG-2 (c) and N-CDs/3DG-3 (d); TEM (e) and AFM (f) images of N-CDs/3DG-2. Inset in f shows the particle size distribution of immobilized N-CDs on graphene nanosheets.

(a) XRD patterns of N-CDs, 3DG and N-CDs/3DG. (b) Raman spectra of 3DG, and N-CDs/3DG. (c) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and (d) pore-size distribution curves calculated from the adsorption branch of the isotherms by Non-local density function theory method for 3DG and N-CDs/3DG.

(a) The survey XPS spectra for N-CDs, 3DG, N-CDs/3DG, (b) high-resolution C 1s spectra of N-CDs/3DG-2. High-resolution N 1s spectra for (c) N-CDs, (d) N-CDs/3DG-1, (e) N-CDs/3DG-2, and (f) N-CDs/3DG-3.

The electrochemical performances of the as-prepared N-CDs. (a) CV curves at different scan rates and (b) GCD plots at various current densities of N-CDs. (c) Rate capability and (d) cycling stability at 1 A g−1 of N-CDs and CDs, (e) Nyquist plots and (f) Bode curves of N-CDs and CDs, the inset in (e) shows the magnified curves at the high frequency.

Electrochemical performance of the as-resulted 3DG, N-CDs/3DG. (a) CV curves at 50 mV s−1 and (b) GCD curves at 0.5 A g−1. (c) CV curves at different scan rates and (d) GCD curves at different densities of N-CDs/3DG-2. (e) specific capacitance and (f) areal capacitance at different current densities of 3DG and N-CDs/3DG electrodes.

The cycling stability of N-CDs, 3DG, and N-CDs/3DG-2. The current density of N-CDs is 1 A g−1 with 5000 charging/discharging cycles, the current density for 3DG and N-CDs/3DG-2 is 10 A g−1 with 20 000 charging/discharging cycles. The inset is the CV curves of N-CDs/3DG-2 at the 1st (black dotted line) and 20 000th (red solid line) cycles, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Nyquist plots and (b) electric conductivity and resistivity of 3DG and N-CDs/3DG. The inset in (a) shows the magnified plots in the higher frequency region. (c) Plots for the dependence of q−1 vs V−1/2 and (d) dependence of q vs V−1/2 for 3DG and N-CDs/3DG.

head.ce:title: Small nitrogen-doped carbon dots as efficient nanoenhancer for boosting the electrochemical performance of three-dimensional graphene

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Small nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are employed as efficient nanoenhancer to significantly boost the electrochemical performances of three-dimensional graphene (3DG) for supercapacitors.

As a new class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots have triggered intensive research interest in various fields. However, the low surface area, hydrophilicity, and agglomeration characteristics limit their applications in energy storage fields. Herein, we demonstrate that nitrogen-doped carbon dots can be employed as efficient nanoenhancer to boost the electrochemical performance of three-dimensional graphene. The as-prepared materials exhibit an interconnected framework with abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups, which enable fast penetration and transport of electrolyte ions and provide more active sites and electric conductivity. Employed as binder-free electrode for supercapacitors, the resultant materials present high specific capacitance (338 F g−1) and areal capacitance (604 μF cm−2) at a current density of 0.5 A g−1, which is much higher than that of pristine three-dimensional graphene (190 F g−1, and 114 μF cm−2), with an enhancement of 78% and 430%, respectively. Moreover, superior long-term cycling stability (94% of capacitance retention after 20 000 charging/discharging cycles at 10 A g−1) as well as improved electric conductivity can also be achieved. These results certify that nitrogen-doped carbon dots can be applied as nanobooster to comprehensively improve the performance of graphene for high-performance electrochemical energy storage.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the problems of the rapid energy consumption and seriously environmental pollution, advanced energy conversion and storage devices and systems, such as lithium-ion batteries, supercapacitors, and fuel cells, have been broadly perused [1–3]. Among these energy conversion and storage devices, supercapacitors have attracted great attention because of the high power density, long cycling performance, and rapid charging/discharging rate [4,5]. It has been demonstrated that electrode material is the critical factor to dominate the electrochemical performance of supercapacitors [3]. Carbon-based micro-/nano-structured materials, including activated carbons [6], carbon aerogels [7], carbon nanotube [8], and graphene [9], have been emerged as electrode materials for supercapacitors due to their high surface area, outstanding electrical conductivity, and excellent stability [10].

As a result of its high theoretical specific surface area (2630 m2 g−1), outstanding electronic conductivity, excellent mechanical and thermal properties, graphene has aroused increasing interest in the fields of electrochemical energy storage and conversion [11–13]. Nevertheless, the serious restacking and aggregation of graphene nanosheets always lead to the low specific capacitance (far less than its theoretical value of 550 F g−1) and poor rate capability [14]. To address these issues, considerable efforts have been devoted to introducing nanoparticles into graphene nanosheets or building three-dimensional interconnected network to alleviate the restacking of graphene [15–18]. For example, Xu et al. prepared graphene oxide quantum dots decorating reduced graphene oxide (rGO) with high nitrogen content by photo reduction method [15]. Zhao et al. prepared nitrogen-doped graphene with three-dimensional (3D) framework to mitigate the restacking and improve the electrochemical performance of graphene [16]. However, the capacitance, rate capability, and stability of graphene-based materials are still far from the needs of practical applications. To design and realize novel nanoarchitectures with comprehensively improved performance of graphene is still a great challenge, especially for practical electrochemical energy storage applications.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have been intensively investigated as multifunctional materials in various fields of chemistry, agriculture, materials, and biology owing to the low toxicity [19], low cost [20], biocompatibility [21], tunable photoluminescence and small size [22]. More recently, CDs were successfully employed to fabricate nanocomposites with improved performance for catalysis, sensor, and photoelectric conversion [22–27]. [20]For instance, Zhang et al. demonstrated nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and rGO nanocomposite with enhanced catalytic activity for oxygen reduction [23]. N-GQDs/hydrogenated titanium dioxide composites also exhibited well photocatalytic with Ultraviolet (UV)-visible-Near infrared broad spectrum active photocatalytic property [24]. GQDs have been anchored to phenyl C-61 butyric acid methyl ester, which can improve the charge extraction and decrease the trap state density of perovskite films [28]. Unfortunately, small CDs were rarely applied in the fields of electrochemical energy storage [29,30], which may be due to the poor electrochemical performances resulted from the low specific surface area, hydrophilicity, and easily agglomerated characteristics [22].

Herein, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are employed as an efficient nanoenhancer to comprehensively boost the electrochemical performance of graphene nanosheets with 3D interconnected network for high-performance supercapacitors. Experimental results reveal that N-CDs are well immobilized on the 3D graphene (3DG) networks, which can effectively prevent the restacking and aggregation of graphene nanosheets. When directly used as binder-free electrode for supercapacitors, the as-synthesized N-CDs/3DG exhibits a maximum specific capacitance of 338 F g−1 and high areal capacitance of 604 μF cm−2 at current density of 0.5 A g−1, which are much higher than those of pristine 3DG (i.e., 190 F g−1 and 114 μF cm−2, respectively). A high capacitance retention of 94% after 20 000 charging/discharging cycles at a current density of 10 A g−1 can also be obtained. Additionally, the electric conductivity of N-CDs/3DG is also greatly improved (3 times of the pristine 3DG). These results indicate that the small N-CDs can act a significant role as marvelous nanobooster to dramatically improve the electrochemical performance of graphene-based nanomaterials for supercapacitors.

Citric acid (99.5%), ethylenediamine monohydrate (98%), hydrogen peroxide (30%) and sodium nitrate (99%) were purchased from Aladdin. Potassium permanganate (99.5%), hydrochloric acid (36%), sulfuric acid (98%) and graphite powder were purchased from DAMAO chemical reagent factory. All agents were used as received without further purification.

To synthesize the N-CDs, citric acid (0.42 g) and ethylenediamine monohydrate (536 μL) were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water. Then, the solution was sealed in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 200 °C for 5 h and the heating gradient was 10 °C min−1 [31]. The CDs were synthesized from citric acid by heating at 200 °C for 3 h with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 for comparison [32]. The as-resulted N-CDs were purified by dialysis for 48 h, and then freeze-dried overnight. Subsequently, various amounts of N-CDs were dispersed into 20 mL of deionized water by sonication to form homogeneous suspensions with desired concentration.

Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared from natural graphite powder by a modified Hummers’ method [33]. The 3DG and N-CDs/3DG interconnected framework were obtained through one-step hydrothermal method, as schematically illustrated in Scheme S1 (Supplementary Information). In a typical process, GO (80 mg) was dispersed in 20 mL of N-CDs aqueous suspension with various concentrations of 0, 0.1, 0.25, and 0.5 mg mL−1, then sonicated and stirred for 0.5 h to form a homogeneous suspension. The resultant suspension was sealed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. After cooling to room temperature naturally, all samples were washed with deionized water until the wash fluid was neutral. With respect to the amounts of N-CDs mentioned above, the as-resulted samples were named as 3DG, N-CDs/3DG-1, N-CDs/3DG-2, and N-CDs/3DG-3, respectively.

The morphologies of N-CDs, 3DG and N-CDs/3DG were characterized by the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, ZEISS Ultra 55), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, TECNAI 10), and atomic force microscopy (AFM, VEECO Multimode, America). The fluorescent spectrum was performed by using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer allocated with a monochromator (resolution: 0.2 nm) and a 150 W Xe lamp as the excitation source. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded from an X-ray powder diffractometer (XD-2X/M4600, Cu Kα, PERSEE Beijing, λ = 0.154051 nm) at ambient temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos, Amicus spectrometer) was used to investigate the surface elemental composition and electronic state of the elements in the resultant samples. Raman spectrum was recorded on a Labram-010 microscopic confocal Raman spectrometer with a 633 nm laser excitation. Porosity characteristics of all samples were determined from N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms that was measured at 77 K using an automatic volumetric sorption analyzer (ASAP 2020 Micromeritics sorption analyzer). The specific surface area was calculated from the adsorption isotherms by using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The electric conductivity was measured by the four probe powder conductivity tester (FT-341A).

The electrochemical measurements were conducted in a three-electrode cell with 6.0 M KOH aqueous solution as electrolyte at room temperature (25 °C). The as-resulted 3DG and N-CDs/3DG were pressed into a thin slice and cut it into a square with the length of 1 cm. Then, the cut slice was pressed onto nickel foam (current collector) as the working electrode, and the mass loading of active materials was about 2–2.5 mg cm−2 for each working electrode. The working electrode of N-CDs was manufactured by mixing N-CDs, PTFE binder, and carbon black with a mass ratio of 8:1:1 in ethanol to form slurry. Then, the slurry was painted on the nickel foam, dried at temperature of 105 °C for 12 h, and pressed into a sheet under pressure of 10 MPa. The mass loading of N-CDs was approximately 2.5 mg cm−2. A Pt plate (1.5 × 1.5 cm−2) and Hg/HgO electrode were used to counter and reference electrode, respectively.

To evaluate the electrochemical performances of all samples, cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD), and cycling stability were tested on the CHI660e electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Instructment Co., Shanghai). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed at the open-circuit potential on the electrochemical system (Zahnex Corp), in which the frequency range was from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz with an alternating current perturbation of 5 mV.

The gravimetric specific capacitance based on the weight of the active material was calculated from the CV and GCD curves according to the following equations:

(A) is the specific capacitance and instant current on CV curves, respectively.

(V s−1) refers to the scan rate of CV curves,

(g) is the mass of the active material loaded on the electrode, while

(V) and
U 1
(V) is the high and low potential limits of the CV measurement.

I × Δ t

(3)

sa

C g
SA
where Cg (F g−1) is the gravimetric specific capacitance calculated from the GCD curves.
I
(A),
Δ t
(s),
m
(g) and
Δ V
(V) which are corresponding to constant current, discharge time, mass loading of the active material on the electrode, and the potential window, respectively. Csa (μF cm−2) refers to the areal specific capacitance per specific surface area (m2 g−1) of the as-prepared samples [34,35].

Fig. 1 a shows photoluminescence spectra of the as-prepared N-CDs suspension. A typical excitation-wavelength-dependent photoluminescence characteristic of the N-CDs can be observed clearly. As shown in Fig. 1b, the N-CDs have optimal emission and excitation wavelengths at 430 and 350 nm, and a blue color is shown under a UV light of 365 nm in inset Fig. 1b [31]. Fig. 1c and d display the TEM and AFM images of N-CDs, respectively. Fig. 1c depicts that N-CDs are homogeneously dispersed in deionized water and the inset in Fig. 1c shows the HRTEM image of N-CDs. A lattice fringe of 0.28 nm can be seen, which is corresponding to the interplanar spacing of (1 0 0) for graphite. The particle sizes of the as-prepared N-CDs are approximately 1–3 nm (inset in Fig. 1d). It can also be observed that some N-CDs are aggregated into large nanoparticles due to the easily agglomerated nature.

The morphologies and micro-structures of 3DG and N-CDs/3DG were characterized by FESEM, TEM, and AFM. Fig. 2 a-d depicts the FESEM images of 3DG and N-CDs/3DG nanocomposites. It can be seen that 3DG and N-CDs/3DG present an interconnected 3D network. The serious restacking of graphene nanosheets are effectively alleviated by constructing a 3D interconnected framework. The immobilization of N-CDs between graphene nanosheets can further mitigate the aggregation, which can facilitate the ion transport of electrolyte. TEM image shown in Fig. 2e reveals a typical structure of wrinkled nanosheets for the resultant N-CDs/3DG. Small N-CDs with size of 2–5 nm are well-anchored on graphene nanosheets. Fig. 2f presents the two dimensional AFM image of the as-obtained N-CDs/3DG-2. It can be observed that the N-CDs are immobilized on graphene nanosheets, and the topographic height of the N-CDs on 3DG nanosheets range from 2 to 4 nm (inset in Fig. 2f), which is well consistent to TEM observations. Moreover, compared with the pristine N-CDs shown in Fig. 1d, the aggregation of N-CDs can also be effectively alleviated by the immobilization on graphene nanosheets (Fig. 2f).

The XRD (Fig. 3 a) pattern of N-CDs presents a broad peak centered at 25.3° (∼0.336 nm), which may be assigned to highly defected or disordered carbons [31]. Notably, the XRD patterns for 3DG and N-CDs/3DG present two peaks centered at 23.8° and 24.6°, respectively. The interlayer space of N-CDs/3DG (∼0.345 nm) is smaller than that of pristine 3DG (∼0.357 nm), which may be attributed to the chemical bonds and hydrogen bonds (OHN, OHO, NHO) between N-CDs and graphene nanosheets, as schematically illustrated in Fig. S1 (Supplementary Information). It can also be observed that the width of these peaks increase along with the concentration of N-CDs, suggesting the increase of the defected or disordered structure in graphene nanosheets. These disordered structures or defects can provide more active sites of N-CDs/3DG for electrochemistry [36]. As depicted in the Raman spectra of 3DG and N-CDs/3DG (Fig. 3b), two peaks around 1350 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 can be attributed to the disordered carbon or defective graphitic structure (d-band) and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in graphene (G-band) [17]. The intensity ratios between D and G band (ID/IG) for 3DG, N-CDs/3DG-1, N-CDs/3DG-2, N-CDs/3DG-3 are about 1.00, 0.99, 0.97 and 0.98, respectively, indicating a similar graphitization degree when anchored N-CDs on 3DG nanosheets.

The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (Fig. 3c) reveal that 3DG exhibits a strong adsorption while N-CDs/3DG is almost non-absorbable when the relative pressure of P/P0 < 0.05. It can be seen that 3DG shows a type IV isotherm with a characteristic hysteresis loop of mesopores, which is related to the capillary condensation in the mesopores. And the N-CDs/3DG displays the isotherms of style III. N-CDs/3DG presents slowly N2 absorption at the relative pressure of P/P0 < 0.9, while a lot of nitrogen absorption in the relative pressure of P/P0 > 0.9 can be noticed [37]. It can be easily observed that 3DG mainly consists of micropores and mesopores and N-CDs/3DG dominates with mesopores and macropores (Fig. 3d). As shown in Table S1 (Supplementary Information), the specific surface areas of 3DG, N-CDs/3DG-1, N-CDs/3DG-2, N-CDs/3DG-3 are 166, 58, 56, and 60 m2 g−1, and the total pore volumes of which are 0.14, 0.14, 0.15, and 0.16 cm3 g−1, respectively.

As shown in the survey XPS spectra (Fig. 4 a), the 3DG displays two visibly peaks center at 285 and 532 eV, which can be attributed to C 1 s and O 1s, respectively. The N-CDs and N-CDs/3DG present three obvious peaks around 285, 399 and 532 eV, corresponding to the C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. As listed in Table S1 (Supplementary Information), the contents of nitrogen for N-CDs, N-CDs/3DG-1, N-CDs/3DG-2, and N-CDs/3DG-3 are 14.47, 1.52, 2.27, and 2.35 at%, respectively, indicating a relatively high nitrogen content in N-CDs/3DG, which can improve the wettability and electrical conductivity in supercapacitors [16].

As depicted in Fig. 4b-f and S2a-d (Supplementary Information), high-resolution C 1s and N 1s spectra were further deconvoluted. High-resolution C 1s spectra can be splitted to five peaks at 284.9, 286, 286.7, 287.6, and 289 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN, CO, CO, and OC-O, respectively [6]. Compared to the pristine 3DG, the peak centers at 286 eV in high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 4b and S1c, d, Supplementary Information) of N-CDs/3DG can be observed because of the nitrogen doping, which can improve the electric conductivity and electrochemical performance of carbon materials [38]. The high-resolution O 1s spectra of N-CDs/3DG at 531.6, 533.4 and 536 eV can be assigned to CO, COC/COH and OCO, respectively, revealing the existence of abundant oxygen functionalities (Fig. S2e-i, Supplementary Information). As shown in Fig. 4c-f, the high-resolution N 1s spectra of N-CDs and N-CDs/3DG can be fitted into three peaks at 399.0, 400.0 and 401.9 eV, which can be attributed to pyridinic N (N-6), pyrrolic N (N-5), and NH groups (N-HX), respectively [39,40]. It should be mentioned that N-6 possesses a larger dipole moment, which can improve the wettability of the material to the electrolyte and reduce the charge transfer resistance between electrolyte and electrode materials [16]. The hydrogen bonds (OHN, OHO, NHO) between N-CDs and graphene nanosheets can shorten the pathway for electron transfer and thus improve the electric conductivity.

To explore the electrochemical performance of N-CDs, the N-CDs-based electrode was tested by CV, GCD, and EIS using 6.0 M KOH aqueous solution as electrolyte, and CDs without nitrogen doping were also investigated for comparison. As shown in the CV curves (Fig. 5 a), the resultant N-CDs present a strong polarization at the potential windows at −0.9–0.1 V, indicating the relatively poor electrochemical performance for N-CDs. The non-linear GCD curves (Fig. 5b) show primarily irreversible capacitance, revealing a pseudocapacitive behavior of N-CDs in aqueous electrolyte [41]. The specific capacitances of N-CDs and CDs calculated from GCD plots are as low as 4.1 and 2.7 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1, respectively, as well as 1.8 and 1.6 F g−1 at a current density of 2 A g−1 (Fig. 5c), suggesting the poor rate capabilities of 44 and 59% for N-CDs and CDs, respectively.

After 5 000 charging/discharging cycles at a current density of 1 A g−1, the specific capacitance retentions are 72 and 75% for N-CDs and CDs (Fig. 5d), respectively, indicating the inferior cycling stability. Fig. 5e and f display the Nyquist plots and Bode curves, respectively. It can be seen that the equivalent series resistances (ESR) of N-CDs and CDs are 0.29 and 0.26 Ω, respectively. The low ESR suggests a low ionic resistance of the electrolyte, intrinsic resistance of the electrode material, and contact resistance from the active material and current collector, which may be contributed from the excellent wettability for the abundant surface functional groups [42]. No semicircle at high frequency could be identified, manifesting the negligible contributions of the electric double layer capacitance and charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the faraday reactions in N-CDs and CDs [43]. The phase angles for N-CDs and CDs are only 37° and 29° at the frequency of 0.01 Hz, respectively, indicating a non-ideal capacitive behavior as well (Fig. 5f). These results are in good agreement with the CV curves and GCD plots.

From the above results, it can be concluded that both N-CDs and CDs present extremely disillusionary electrochemical performances, including quite low specific capacitance, weak rate capability as well as poor cycling stability. Fortunately, we herein first employed small N-CDs as an efficient nanoenhancer to comprehensively boost the electrochemical performances of graphene with 3D interconnected framework. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the CV curves of the pristine 3DG exhibits a quasi-rectangular shape which should be contributed to the double effect of double layer capacitance and redox reactions with oxygen-containing functional groups. After immobilized with small N-CDs between graphene nanosheets, the as-resultant N-CDs/3DG exhibits quite obvious hump in the charging/discharging process, which can be assigned to the pseudocapacitance because of the nitrogen- and oxygen- containing functional groups [16,18]. A slightly deformed triangle-like shape further certifies the existence of the pseudocapacitance in the resultant samples (Fig. 6b). The specific capacitances of 3DG, N-CDs/3DG-1, N-CDs/3DG-2, and N-CDs/3DG-3 calculated from the GCD curves are 190, 259, 338, and 263 F g−1 at the current density of 0.5 A g−1, respectively. Compared with the specific capacitance of pristine 3DG, a great enhancement of 36%, 78%, and 38% for N-CDs/3DG-1, N-CDs/3DG-2, and N-CDs/3DG-3, respectively, can be achieved, implying that the immobilized small N-CDs play a vital role to enhance the electrochemical performance of 3DG. Interestingly, the specific capacitance of N-CDs/3DG is much higher than the sum of specific capacitance of the pristine 3DG and pure N-CDs, suggesting a great positive synergistic effect between 3DG and N-CDs on the improvement of electrochemical performance. In addition, the N-CDs/3DG-2 exhibits a high coulombic efficiency of 117% at 0.5 A g−1, which indicates the high energy available efficiency in the charging/discharging process caused by the increased conductivity, heteroatom doping and interconnected 3D framework [44]. It should be noted that the coulombic efficiency cannot fully delegate energy efficiency [45,46], and the calculated energy efficiencies of N-CDs/3DG-2 are 75% and 77% at the current density of 0.5 A g−1 and 10 A g−1, respectively. These results indicate the elevated energy efficiency and good rate capability when increasing the current density.

The CV curves of N-CDs/3DG-2 displays a slight declination at a high scan rate of 200 mV s−1 (Fig. 6c), suggesting the good charge transfer capability of the resultant N-CDs/3DG. Fig. 6d depicts the GCD curves of N-CDs/3DG-2 at various current densities. The calculated specific capacitances are 338, 296, 276, 260, 255, and 254 F g−1 at distinct current densities of 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, and 20 A g−1, respectively. Within the current density range of 0.5–20 A g−1, the rate capabilities of 3DG, N-CDs/3DG-1, N-CDs/3DG-2, and N-CDs/3DG-3 are 72%, 78%, 75%, and 79%, respectively (Fig. 6e and S3, Supplementary Information). It is obvious that the rate capability of N-CDs/3DG is better than that of pristine 3DG, which may be mainly attributed to the outstanding electric conductivity and the interconnected 3D structure.

Areal capacitance is an important parameter for evaluating the practical application of electrochemical energy storage devices [34]. Assuming the pristine 3DG contributes to the surface area of 160 m2 g−1, the interface area of 3DG electrode should be 160 m2 g−1 as well. Therefore, the areal capacitance of the pristine 3DG normalized by the accessible interfacial surface is ca. 114 μF cm−2 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1. The normalized areal capacitances of N-CDs/3DG-1, N-CDs/3DG-2, and N-CDs/3DG-3 are ca. 446, 604, and 439 μF cm−2 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 (Fig. 6f and Table S2, Supplementary Information), revealing a dramatically improvement of 291%, 430%, and 285% compared with that of the pristine 3DG (114 μF cm−2). These results indicate the enhanced interaction between the electrolyte ions and graphene nanosheets, leading to a significantly increased utilization of the effective surface area for the as-prepared N-CDs/3DG. The higher utilization of the effective electrochemical surface area for graphene nanosheets can improve the formation of the electric double layer [34]. Therefore, the as-prepared N-CDs/3DG with lower specific surface area exhibit a superior electrochemical performance to the pristine 3DG with higher specific surface area.

The long-term cycling stability of the CDs, 3DG and N-CDs/3DG-2 were analyzed by the GCD measurements (Fig. 7 ). After 20 000 charging/discharging cycles at a high current density of 10 A g−1, the specific capacitance of N-CDs/3DG-2 decreases from 242 to 227F g−1, while that of 3DG decreases from 144 to 129 F g−1. The capacitance retentions of N-CDs, 3DG, and N-CDs/3DG-2 are 72%, 89%, and 94%, respectively, indicating the cycling stability of N-CDs/3DG-2 is superior to those of 3DG and N-CDs. The improvement of cycling stability for N-CDs/3DG-2 can be attributed to the synergistic effect between 3DG and N-CDs. It should be noticed that the specific capacitance loss of 6% for N-CDs-3DG-2 is mainly assigned to the first 5 000 cycles (ca. 5%) and only 1% of the decrease at the last 15,000 cycles. These results suggest that the specific capacitance loss of N-CDs/3DG-2 may be mainly attributed to the loss of pseudocapacitance.

The inset in Fig. 7 shows the CV curves at different charging/discharging cycles. In the first charging/discharging cycle, the pseudocapacitance and double layer capacitance are the total capacitance of 24.2% and 75.8%, respectively. After 20,000 charging/discharging cycles, the pseudocapacitance and double layer capacitance are 15.3% and 84.7% of the total capacitance (Table S3, Supplementary Information), implying that the capacitance loss is mainly due to the loss of pseudocapacitance resulted from the irreversible Faradic reaction of N- and O-containing functional groups. In comparison with previously reported graphene-based electrode materials decorated with another quantum dots or nanoparticles (Table S4, Supplementary Information) [15,18,29,47-49], the as-fabricated N-CDs/3DG exhibits competitive electrochemical performances including higher specific capacitance, better rate capability, and longer cycling stability, highlighting the great potential of the resultant N-CDs/3DG for electrochemical energy storage.

As shown in Fig. 8 a, the ESR of the pristine 3DG and N-CDs/3DG are 1.1 and 0.40 Ω, respectively, indicating the decreased resistance of 3DG framework when immobilized with small N-CDs. Meanwhile, the Rct of N-CDs/3DG is 1.1 Ω, which is much lower than that of the pristine 3DG (2.2 Ω). It can be noticed that the Rct of N-CDs/3DG decreases 50% of the pristine 3DG, suggesting a much faster electron transportation of the resultant N-CDs/3DG than that of the pristine 3DG. Furthermore, the electrical conductivities of 3DG, N-CDs/3DG-1, N-CDs/3DG-2, N-CDs/3DG-3 are ca. 26.3, 55.4, 77.1, and 44.8 S m−1 (Fig. 8b), respectively. Obviously, the electrical conductivities of N-CDs/3DG-1, N-CDs/3DG-2, N-CDs/3DG-3 are ∼2.1, 3.0, and 1.7 times higher than that of the pristine 3DG, respectively, revealing a great improvement of electric conductivity via immobilizing small N-CDs between graphene nanosheets. Nitrogen can act as an electron-donor and simultaneously N-CDs can serve as a “bridge” to shorten the pathway for electron transfer to improve the electric conductivity [47].

To further investigate the important effect of small N-CDs for the electrochemical performance on 3DG network, the contribution of double layer capacitance (Cdl) and pseudocapacitance capacitance (Cp) to the as-prepared N-CDs/3DG were evaluated [50-52]. The curves of q−1 versus V1/2 and q versus V−1/2 which based on the CV curves at various scan rates are shown in Fig. 8c and d, respectively, in which q and V are the voltammetry charge and scan voltage, respectively. The total voltammetry charge (qT) and double layer voltammetry charge (qdl) can be acquired from the intercept of Y-axis by extending the fitted linear for each electrode shown in Fig. 8c and d, respectively. The pseudocapacitance voltammetry charge (qp) can be obtained by the different values between qT and qdl. The total specific capacitance (CS,T), Cdl and Cp can be received by dividing the diverse scan voltages, and the results are listed in Table S5 (Supplementary Information). It can be observed that both Cdl and Cp of N-CDs/3DG are much higher than that of the pristine 3DG, suggesting the immobilized small N-CDs can effectively improve both the double layer capacitance and pseudocapacitance.

On the basis of these results, it can be concluded that small N-CDs can be employed as an efficient nanoenhancer to comprehensively and dramatically boost the electrochemical performances of graphene with 3D framework. The dramatic improvement of N-CDs/3DG may be assigned to the positive synergistic effect of the N-CDs and 3DG network. Firstly, the small N-CDs immobilized between graphene nanosheets can effectively prevent the restacking of graphene nanosheets, which increase the utilization of inner surface and effective specific surface, and enable the N-CDs/3DG to more fully contact with electrolyte and reduce the resistance. Secondly, the abundant nitrogen-containing functional groups of N-CDs/3DG can strengthen the pseudocapacitance and electric conductivity. Thirdly, the hydrogen bonds between small N-CDs and graphene nanosheets act as a “bridge”, which can connect the graphene nanosheets, provide fast pathway for electrons to shuttle the graphene interlayers, and improve the electrochemical stability. Finally, the small N-CDs are well anchored on the graphene nanosheets, efficiently inhibiting the agglomeration of N-CDs, which further enhance the structure stability during charging/discharging process.

To be summarized, small N-CDs were employed as an efficient nanoenhancer for dramatically boosting the electrochemical performance of 3DG, in spite of the depressed electrochemical property of pure N-CDs. Impressively, the small N-CDs were well anchored on the graphene nanosheets, which can inhibit the agglomeration both of N-CDs and 3DG to increase the effective utilization of specific surface area. Furthermore, the hydrogen bonds between small N-CDs and graphene nanosheets served as a “bridge” which can interconnect the graphene nanosheets, provide fast path for electron transfer, and improve the electrochemical stability. The as-resulted N-CDs/3DG exhibited a high specific capacitance (338 F g−1) and ultrahigh areal capacitance (604 μF cm−2), with a great enhancement of 78% and 430% to that of the pristine 3DG (190 g−1 and 114 μF cm−2). In comparison with those state-of-the-art functionalized graphene-based electrode materials reported in literatures [15,18,29,47–49], our N-CDs/3DG displayed comprehensively and significantly enhanced electrochemical performances including capacitance, rate capability, cycling stability, and conductivity. Our method was simple and highly efficient, which can be expanded to fabricate CDs immobilized 2D nanomaterials such as metal oxides, layered double hydroxides, sulfides, and Mxenes, showing great promises for electrochemical energy storage and conversion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21571066, U1501242, 51602107, and 21671069), the Key Program of Science Technology Innovation Foundation of Universities (cxzd1113), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (S2013030012842), and Guangdong Science and Technology Planning Project (2015A020209147, and 201704030022) in China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.10.096.

ce:section.ce:para:
